statement: Soccer is played with 10 players on each side.
explanation: Each team has 11 players on the field, including the goalkeeper.

statement: A soccer match is 90 minutes long, played in two halves.
explanation: Two halves of 45 minutes with a 15-minute halftime break.

statement: The World Cup happens every 2 years.
explanation: The World Cup runs on a 4-year cycle.

statement: The 2026 World Cup is hosted only by the United States.
explanation: 2026 is the first World Cup with three host countries: the US, Canada, and Mexico.

statement: Soccer is called football in most of the world.
explanation: The name soccer is mainly used in the US, Canada, and Australia.

statement: A hat-trick means scoring 3 goals in one match.
explanation: When a single player scores three times in one game, it's called a hat-trick.

statement: The pitch is what soccer fans call the playing field.
explanation: Pitch is the standard word for the soccer field in most of the world.

statement: Each goal scored counts as one point.
explanation: There is no extra value for distance or difficulty; every goal is worth 1.

statement: A yellow card means the player has to leave the game.
explanation: Yellow is a warning. A red card, or two yellows in the same match, forces the player off the field for the rest of the game.

statement: Any player can use their hands to move the ball during play.
explanation: Only the goalkeeper can use their hands, and only inside their own penalty area.

statement: A penalty kick is a one-on-one against the goalkeeper, taken from a marked spot.
explanation: From 12 yards (11 meters) out. Just the kicker vs the keeper.

statement: The team captain wears an armband to show their role.
explanation: The captain's armband is worn on the upper arm and identifies the team leader on the field.

statement: The goalkeeper can use their hands anywhere on the field.
explanation: Goalkeepers can only use their hands inside their own penalty area.

statement: When the ball goes out on the side of the field, the player throwing it back in must use both hands.
explanation: This is called a throw-in. Both hands, and both feet on the ground behind the line.

statement: Brazil has won the World Cup more times than any other country.
explanation: Brazil has 5 World Cup titles, more than any other nation.

statement: Argentina won the most recent World Cup.
explanation: Argentina won the 2022 final in Qatar, beating France on penalties.

statement: Every foul in a soccer match leads to a yellow card.
explanation: Most fouls are just free kicks. Yellow and red cards are reserved for more serious offences or repeat fouls.

statement: There are 48 teams in the 2026 World Cup.
explanation: The 2026 tournament expanded from 32 to 48 teams, the most in World Cup history.

statement: The World Cup tournament lasts about a year.
explanation: The tournament runs for roughly a month, from group stage to final.

statement: Mexico is hosting the World Cup for the third time in 2026.
explanation: Mexico hosted in 1970 and 1986, making 2026 its third turn as host.

statement: The 2026 World Cup final will be played in Los Angeles.
explanation: The final is at MetLife Stadium in the New York / New Jersey area.

statement: The Golden Boot is awarded to the World Cup's top goal scorer.
explanation: Whoever scores the most goals across the tournament wins the Golden Boot.

statement: Some World Cup games are allowed to end in a tie.
explanation: Early-round games can end in a draw. Later, single-elimination games must produce a winner, with extra time and penalty kicks if needed.

statement: Soccer is the most popular sport in the world by audience.
explanation: Billions of people follow soccer globally, more than any other sport.

statement: The 2026 World Cup tournament begins in June.
explanation: The tournament opens on June 11, 2026 at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City.

statement: The 2026 World Cup will end in August.
explanation: The final is on July 19, 2026 at MetLife Stadium.

statement: Atlanta is one of the host cities for the 2026 World Cup.
explanation: Mercedes-Benz Stadium in Atlanta hosts matches in 2026.

statement: Chicago is one of the host cities for the 2026 World Cup.
explanation: Chicago is not on the 2026 host list. The closest Midwest host is Kansas City.

statement: All host cities for the 2026 World Cup are in the United States.
explanation: The 16 host cities are split across the US, Canada (Toronto and Vancouver), and Mexico (Mexico City, Guadalajara, Monterrey).

statement: There will be more than 100 matches in the 2026 World Cup.
explanation: 104 matches in total, up from 64 in 2022.

statement: A team can make up to 5 substitutions during a World Cup match.
explanation: The 5-substitution rule was made permanent ahead of the 2022 World Cup.

statement: A clean sheet means the goalkeeper let in only one goal.
explanation: A clean sheet means zero goals allowed. It's soccer's term for a shutout.

statement: VAR stands for Video Assistant Referee.
explanation: VAR reviews key decisions like goals, penalties, and red cards.

statement: "Nil" means zero in soccer.
explanation: A 1-0 win is often called "one-nil."

statement: A tie game is worth nothing to either team in the World Cup standings.
explanation: A tie (a "draw") gives each team 1 point. A win is 3 points, a loss is 0.

statement: If a must-have-a-winner game is tied after 90 minutes, the teams play an extra 30 minutes.
explanation: This is called "extra time": two 15-minute halves added on.

statement: If a must-have-a-winner game is still tied after extra time, the two teams take turns shooting penalty kicks.
explanation: Each team takes 5 penalty kicks; if still tied, sudden-death penalties follow.

statement: A striker is mainly a defensive position.
explanation: Strikers are forwards. Their main job is to score goals.

statement: Stoppage time is extra time added at the end of each half.
explanation: The referee adds time to make up for stoppages like injuries and substitutions.

statement: The country that wins the World Cup keeps the actual trophy forever.
explanation: The champion holds the real trophy until the next tournament and keeps a replica.

statement: The 2026 World Cup opening match is in the United States.
explanation: The opener is at Estadio Azteca in Mexico City on June 11, 2026.

statement: Las Vegas is a 2026 World Cup host city.
explanation: Las Vegas is not a 2026 host. The Western US hosts are Los Angeles, San Francisco Bay Area, and Seattle.

statement: The 2026 World Cup final will be played at MetLife Stadium.
explanation: MetLife in East Rutherford, New Jersey hosts the final on July 19, 2026.

statement: A midfielder plays between the defense and the attack.
explanation: Midfielders link defenders and forwards, often controlling the pace of play.

statement: A team has more than one goalkeeper on the field at the same time.
explanation: Only one goalkeeper plays at once. Backup keepers sit on the bench.

statement: Boston is one of the 2026 World Cup host cities.
explanation: Gillette Stadium in Foxborough hosts matches for the Boston area.

statement: Denver is one of the 2026 World Cup host cities.
explanation: Denver is not a 2026 host. The 11 US host cities don't include any in the Mountain West.

statement: The 2022 World Cup was held in Brazil.
explanation: The 2022 World Cup was in Qatar, the first Middle Eastern host country.

statement: A 0-0 match is often called a "nil-nil draw."
explanation: Nil-nil is the standard way to read a scoreless draw aloud.
